message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "How does AGE affect the ability to comprehend ACCELERATED / TIME-COMPRESSED / fast speech (i.e. "speed listening")? Cover the full human lifespan across these angles: (1) DEVELOPMENTAL curve in childhood and adolescence — at what age does the ability to understand time-compressed/fast speech mature, and how do children compare to young adults? (2) AGING decline — how does comprehension of time-compressed speech change in older adults (50s through 80s), how steep is the decline, and at what age does it begin? Give quantitative thresholds (syllables/second, words/minute, or % time-compression) by age band wherever available. (3) MECHANISMS — disentangle peripheral hearing loss (presbycusis) from central/cognitive factors: central auditory temporal processing, cognitive/perceptual processing speed, and working-memory / phonological-loop capacity; which factor dominates the age effect, and does the age penalty persist after controlling for audibility? (4) PEAK age — at what age is accelerated-speech comprehension at its maximum, and how wide is the plateau? (5) TRAINABILITY / PLASTICITY across age — can older adults still train to improve speed-listening comprehension and by how much, and how does AGE OF ONSET of blindness (early-blind vs late-blind) modulate the ultra-fast-speech advantage and its neural (visual-cortex) plasticity? CONTEXT: this extends prior research on speed-listening; relate findings to a healthy adult who currently comprehends roughly 13 syllables/second of time-compressed audiobook narration. Prefer peer-reviewed sources; provide quantitative figures and effect sizes by age group where possible, and flag where peripheral hearing loss is or isn't controlled for."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6364265/
**Title:** Time-Compressed Speech Identification Is Predicted by Auditory Neural Processing, Perceptuomotor Speed, and Executive Functioning in Younger and Older Listeners (Smith et al.)
**Found via:** Quantitative thresholds: syllables/sec, WPM, % compression by age band search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the source and extract relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract all quantitative findings about how AGE affects comprehension/identification of time-compressed (fast) speech. Specifically capture: (1) any age-band differences between younger and older listeners in time-compressed speech identification scores; (2) the compression ratios/rates used (e.g., % time-compression, syllables/sec, words/min); (3) which predictors (auditory neural processing, perceptuomotor speed, executive functioning, hearing thresholds) explained performance and their effect sizes/correlations; (4) whether the age penalty persists after controlling for peripheral hearing/audibility; (5) mechanisms distinguishing peripheral hearing loss from central/cognitive factors. Provide direct verbatim quotes for each finding, the study sample (ages, N), publication date, journal, and authors. Quote exact statistics where present.

content.content:
# Age Effects on Time-Compressed Speech Identification

**Study Details:**
- **Authors:** Dias, McClaskey, Harris
- **Journal:** Journal of the Association for Research in Otolaryngology
- **Publication:** 2018 Nov 19; 20(1):73–88
- **Sample:** 21 younger adults (ages 19-30) + 30 older adults (ages 56-82)

---

## (1) Age-Band Differences in Identification Scores

**40% compression:** No significant difference
> "no age-group difference was found for 40 % time-compressed speech"

**50% compression:** 
> "Older listeners had more difficulty accurately identifying 50 and 60 % time-compressed speech"
- Younger: 0.868 accuracy | Older: 0.795 accuracy | *t*(49)=2.725, *p*=0.009

**60% compression:**
- Younger: 0.740 accuracy | Older: 0.537 accuracy | *t*(49)=4.859, *p*<0.001
> "Compared to younger adults, the identification scores of older adults were significantly lower"

---

## (2) Compression Ratios Used

> "40, 50, and 60 % time-compressed sentences"

**Original speech rate:** Approximately 200 words per minute

Time compression method:
> "Periodic information was uniformly removed from the untransformed acoustic signals"

---

## (3) Independent Predictors & Effect Sizes

**P1 Peak Latency** (auditory neural processing):
- Standardized β = −0.181, *t*(46)=−3.045, *p*=0.004
> "Listeners with longer P1 peak latencies exhibited poorer time-compressed speech identification"

**Perceptuomotor Processing Speed** (Connections Simple):
- Standardized β = 0.242, *t*(46)=3.028, *p*=0.004
> "Listeners with faster perceptuomotor processing exhibited better time-compressed speech identification"

**Executive Functioning** (residualized Connections Complex):
- Standardized β = 0.132, *t*(46)=2.226, *p*=0.031
> "Listeners with more efficient executive functioning exhibited better time-compressed speech identification"

All three predictors remained independent in combined model (Model 4).

---

## (4) Age Penalty Persists Beyond Peripheral Hearing

> "audiometric thresholds do not mediate the relationship between age and time-compressed speech identification"

Adding right-ear audiometric thresholds to age/compression rate model:
- χ²(1)=0.130, *p*=0.718 (not significant)
- Audiometric thresholds: *t*(48)=0.350, *p*=0.728 (not significant predictor)

All participants met normal hearing criterion:
> "audiometric thresholds ≤ 25 dB HL between 250 and 3000 Hz"

---

## (5) Mechanisms: Peripheral vs. Central/Cognitive Distinction

**Age-related decline mediated by perceptuomotor speed:**
When perceptuomotor processing added to model with age group:
> "the effect of age group...became a nonsignificant predictor in model 2a. The addition of perceptuomotor processing speed in model 2a mediated the effect of age group"

**P1 latency reflects neural synchrony:**
> "Shorter click-induced P1 latencies, resulting from better myelinization and more synchronous firing of neurons along the auditory path, may facilitate time-compressed speech identification"

**Distinction emphasizing multiple levels:**
> "time-compressed speech identification is subject to both low-level peripheral processing and higher-level perceptuomotor processing speed and executive functioning ability"

content.text: I have enough detail from the source. Note the methods state participants were screened to normal hearing, so peripheral hearing loss is controlled by design. Let me record the structured claims.

claims.claim: Older adults (56-82) identified time-compressed speech significantly worse than younger adults (19-30) at high compression rates: at 60% compression older listeners scored 0.537 vs 0.740 for younger (t(49)=4.859, p<0.001), and at 50% compression 0.795 vs 0.868 (t(49)=2.725, p=0.009), while at 40% there was no age difference.
claims.quote: Older listeners had more difficulty accurately identifying 50 and 60 % time-compressed speech ... Compared to younger adults, the identification scores of older adults were significantly lower ... no age-group difference was found for 40 % time-compressed speech

claims.claim: The age penalty on time-compressed speech identification is NOT explained by peripheral hearing/audibility: all participants had normal audiometric thresholds (≤25 dB HL, 250-3000 Hz), and adding audiometric thresholds did not improve the model (chi-square(1)=0.130, p=0.718; threshold predictor t(48)=0.350, p=0.728).
claims.quote: audiometric thresholds do not mediate the relationship between age and time-compressed speech identification

claims.claim: The age-related decline in time-compressed speech identification was statistically mediated by perceptuomotor (cognitive/perceptual) processing speed: adding perceptuomotor speed to the model rendered the age-group effect nonsignificant, indicating central processing speed rather than age per se drives the deficit.
claims.quote: the effect of age group...became a nonsignificant predictor in model 2a. The addition of perceptuomotor processing speed in model 2a mediated the effect of age group

claims.claim: Three independent factors predicted time-compressed speech identification: auditory neural processing (longer P1 peak latency = worse, beta=-0.181, p=0.004), faster perceptuomotor processing speed (beta=0.242, p=0.004), and better executive functioning (beta=0.132, p=0.031), all remaining significant simultaneously.
claims.quote: time-compressed speech identification is subject to both low-level peripheral processing and higher-level perceptuomotor processing speed and executive functioning ability

claims.claim: Slower central auditory neural synchrony, indexed by P1 latency, predicts poorer fast-speech identification, linking the age effect to neural temporal processing (myelination/synchronous firing) rather than just audibility.
claims.quote: Shorter click-induced P1 latencies, resulting from better myelinization and more synchronous firing of neurons along the auditory path, may facilitate time-compressed speech identification

content.text: Extracted 5 falsifiable claims from this peer-reviewed primary source (Dias, McClaskey & Harris, 2018, J Assoc Res Otolaryngol; note the prompt attributed it to "Smith et al." but the actual authors are Dias et al.). Source is high quality (primary research, normal-hearing-screened sample of 21 younger adults aged 19-30 and 30 older adults aged 56-82). Key relevance to the research question: it directly addresses the AGING decline (angle 2) and MECHANISMS (angle 3), demonstrating that the older-adult deficit on time-compressed speech emerges at higher compression rates, persists after controlling for peripheral audibility, and is statistically mediated by central perceptuomotor processing speed alongside auditory neural synchrony (P1 latency) and executive functioning.
